publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-03-09 23:59:08 UTC.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: Undetermined, Code for undetermined script script.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: Collection consists primarily of correspondence; letters discuss attempts to establish schools for escaped slaves and freedmen in the South, abolition, Oberlin College President Charles Grandison Finney's opinion of John Brown and other abolitionists, the women's suffrage movement (especially in New York State), women's higher education, temperance, the Universal Peace movement, the National Arbitration League of Washington, aspects of Quaker life, the Society of Friends, the Freedmen's Bureau, and other issues.

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Emily Howland was born in Sherwood, New York in 1827, daughter of Slocum and Hanna (Tallcott) Howland. Her parents were prominent in the Society of Friends, and Emily was educated both at a private school in Sherwood and a Friends' school in Philadelphia. Early in life she became an active abolitionist, and during 1857-1859 was a teacher in a school for colored girls in Washington, D.C. In 1863-1864 she worked in a large camp for freed slaves in Arlington, Virginia, teaching them to read and write. With her father's aid, she opened an African-American school in 1867 at Heathsville, Virginia. She continued throughout her life to support this and other African-American schools in the South. In 1882 she took over the Sherwood select school until it was taken over by the New York Board of Regents in 1927. Howland was also active in reform movements such as women's suffrage, and peace and temperance organizations. Her niece, Isabel Howland, daughter of William and Hanna (Letchworth) Howland, also had a lifelong interest in educational and philanthropic activities.

scopecontent.head: Collection Description
scopecontent.p: Collection consists primarily of correspondence; letters discuss attempts to establish schools for escaped enslaved people and freedmen in the South, abolition, Oberlin College President Charles Grandison Finney's opinion of John Brown and other abolitionists, the women's suffrage movement (especially in New York State), women's higher education, temperance, the Universal Peace movement, the National Arbitration League of Washington, aspects of Quaker life, the Society of Friends, the Freedmen's Bureau, and other issues. Correspondents include Isabel Howland, Caroline F. Putnam, Elizabeth Smith Miller, Sallie Holley, Antoinette Brown Blackwell, Cornelia Hancock, Amanda Sanford Hickey, Rev. John D. Read, Emma V. Brown, Booker T. Washington, Herbert Howland (while traveling in England, France, and Egypt), Hanna Letchworth Howland, Joseph and Richard Tallcott, Samuel Parsons, Ebenezer Burnham, George Lincoln Burr (to Isabel Howland), Lillie Devereux Blake, Mary E. Bowman, Gulielma Breed, Phoebe Hathaway, Henry Ince, J. R. Johnson, Alfred H. Love, E. Nash, Amanda T. Jones, Elizabeth Palmer Peabody, L. W. Stebbins, and Julia A. Wilbur. Also includes correspondence of Caroline F. Putnam to Sallie Holley and others.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Emily Howland papers, #2681. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

controlaccess.subject: Philanthropists.

controlaccess.corpname: United States. Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands

did.unittitle: Series I. Emily Howland Correspondence and Papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: See also box 6, folder 13 for the published guide to the microfilm which also contains descriptions of the various periods in Howland's life.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letter from Phoebe Hathaway about raising funds for Chaplin while he was imprisoned, likely referring to William Chaplin who was imprisoned for assisting enslaved people; letters from Henry Ince (1852) regarding the Young Ladies Anti-Slavery Sewing Society and fundraising for escaped enslaved people.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of a letter from Josiah Letchworth about the failed apprehension of an enslaved man from Auburn Prison under the Fugitive Slave Act (March, 1854).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters from Miles S. Griswold discussing politics and slavery and mentioning the Frederick Douglass lecture in Auburn, NY (1856).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from abolitionist Caroline F. Putnam discussing literature; a detailed letter from Putnam describing the lecture of Wendell Phillips about world history and the advancements of civilization; and a letter from Sallie Holley, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from Sallie Holley and Caroline Putnam, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Emma V. Brown discussing her thoughts on slavery (April 8); correspondence with Samuel Rhoads, Gulielma Breed, and Emma V. Brown regarding the building of a new school house by Myrtilla Miner; a letter from Caroline F. Putnam referencing Harriet Tubman as "Moses" and her desire to purchase a home in Auburn (June 18); and correspondence from former students at the Normal School for Colored Girls, among other letters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from students at Oberlin; discussions of Howland starting a new school; discussions of the political situation and Civil War; and correspondence from former students at the Normal School for Colored Girls. Correspondents include Gulielma Breed, Emma V. Brown, and Samantha Nivison among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a copy of a letter from Sayles Jenks Bowen discussing proposed anti-slavery bills, schools for formerly enslaved peoples in Washington, DC, and politics (May 31, 1862); discussions of the political situation and Civil War; a letter from Emma V. Brown describing the news of emancipation in DC and how she had stones thrown at her as she walked to the Normal School for Colored Girls, and another describing a banquet given for "contrabands" (formerly enslaved peoples). Correspondents include Samantha Nivison and Samuel J. May among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a copy of a letter from Howland to her parents, and from Howland to Sarah Baker. Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Sallie Holley, and Amanda Sanford among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence about the Civil War and the formation/function of schools in the north and in Washington DC. Correspondents include Emma V. Brown and Amanda Sanford, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence about the Civil War and politics of Washington, DC, including an incomplete letter describing the moment a petition for Black voting rights passed in Congress, as well as thoughts on President Lincoln and general sentiments towards slavery and formerly enslaved people (February 1865). Letters also include mention of the death of President Lincoln. Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Sallie Holley, J.R. Johnson (regarding schools), Mary Reed, and former school students, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussions of reconstruction, Andrew Johnson, and the Freedmen's Bureau. A photocopy of a letter from Howland to her mother discusses the funeral of Fanny Seward (October 31, 1866). A letter from Sallie G. describes her difficulties with Lieutenant O'Brien, who accused her of sharing a bed with a doctor, and the prejudice she faced against her for her work with the schools. Contains anti-Irish sentiments (February 18). Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Susie Bruce, Thesta Dana, Sallie Holley, J.R. Johnson, Mary Reed, and former students among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Susie Bruce, Thesta Dana, Sarah Goodyear, J.R. Johnson, and Mary Reed, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes several letters from Mary Reed mentioning the reading tour of Charles Dickens and how she wished to see him. Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, F.W. Dow, Sarah Goodyear, J.R. Johnson, William Letchworth, and Mary Reed, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letter from Sallie Holley again asking Howland to allow her name to be published with her letters to the Anti-Slavery Standard, and a letter from S. Stebbins describing two riots, one involving Balagh Haynie (August 13, 1869). Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Sarah Goodyear, J.R. Johnson, Carrie Lacy, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from Susan J. Laber regarding studying medicine at the Woman's Medical College of Pennsylvania in Philadelphia and the women having separate practice and surgery classes than the men. She mentions Dr. Ann Preston. Other correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Sarah Goodyear, Emma L. Haslam, J.R. Johnson, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Sarah Goodyear, Emma L. Haslam, Jane Nash, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of Sidney Taliaferro's report card from Sherwood Academy, as well as correspondence with updates from various schools. Also, a handwritten list of pupils at the Sunday School opened at Howland School, November 9, 1873. Correspondents include J.R. Johnson and Mary Reed, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a leaflet titled "Appeal to the Friends of the Colored People" from Delaware, and a letter from Anna Dorland to Sidney Taliaferro. Correspondents include J.R. Johnson, Mary Reed, and Marcellus Wood among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of a letter from Howland to her father, mentioning the near completion of Ezra Cornell's house, and a letter from Mary Jane in which she discusses taking black children from Sunday School for a ride in Central Park, and her feelings towards Freedmen. Also included are letters from Cornell students, such as Julia Thomas and Susie Hoxie. Correspondents include Lillie Blake, Emma V. Brown, Theodosia G. Chaplin, C.A. Comstock, Sidney Taliaferro, and Mary Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from J.R. Johnson discussing instructing Chinese men in English and Christianity. Correspondents include Lillie Blake, Phebe Coffin, Sallie Holley, Sidney Taliaferro, and Phebe Wood, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter discussing a Wendell Phillips lecture and upcoming Frederick Douglass lecture (January 2, 1877). Correspondents include Sallie Holley, Lydia King, Squire Raymond, Mary Reed, Benjamin Taliaferro, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Lydia King and Mary Reed, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of a letter from Mary Thomas discussing the Exodusters, part of the Exodus of 1879 where thousands of African Americans migrated to Kansas from post-Reconstruction Southern states. Correspondents include Emma V. Brown, Theodosia G. Chaplin, Phebe Coffin, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letter from Catherine discussing Quaker lodges in New York and their acceptance or denial of African American people. Some letters concern George Nash (then General Attorney of Ohio) and possible nonmarital child, the mother of whom was being cared for at a school by Anne Piper. A letter from M. Belle Nelson (February 23) describes the Five Points House of Industry. A letter from Carrie Lacy (August 16) asks for funding for poor African Americans and describes the changing local sentiment as a teacher for black youth. She also mentions her friendship with James Garfield and talks about his honest and fearless character. Mary Thomas writes asking if Harriet Tubman would be willing and able to travel with Elizabeth Comstock to distribute aid to refugees (December 4). Correspondents include Theodosia G. Chaplin, Phebe Coffin, Cornelia Hancock, Lydia King, Abby Munro (discussing mutual acquaintances and affairs), M. Belle Nelson, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others. Also included is a pamphlet on the Concord Summer School of Philosophy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes many condolence letters on the death of Slocum Howland, including a letter from Joseph L. McCoy, the "first colored public school teacher that ever taught in Westmoreland County"; a letter from Sarah Johonnot discussing Cornell University's lack of women on the board of trustees and as professors (August 9); and a letter from Howland to Anne Greene (December 28). Correspondents include Phebe Coffin, Cornelia Hancock, Carrie Lacy, M. Belle Nelson, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others. Also included is a pamphlet on the Concord Summer School of Philosophy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters include discussions of the publication of a biography of Myrtilla Miner, with various people writing their thoughts about her; copies of letters about the origin of Howland School House and Howland Chapel; Universal Peace Union flier and constitution; Miner Normal School graduation pamphlet; condolence letters on the death of Benjamin Howland; a letter from Burt Green Wilder thanking Howland for her letter and offer of a brain, and describing his sadness at having to kill cats and dogs (September 8), and another thanking her for the brain of the dog and asking for more information (September 29); and pamphlets on the Order of Good Templars and the Templar Order with a letter by George H. Fea, arguing for inclusivity of races in temperance lodges. Correspondents include Elizabeth Blakely, Theodosia Chaplin, Phebe Coffin, James Inglish Ferree, Amanda Sanford Hickey, Sallie Holley, H. Hussey, Arthur Kennedy, Abby Munro, Mary Reed, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from D.E. Collins discussing suffrage (December 5). Correspondents include Phebe Coffin, Cornelia Hancock, Lydia King, Mary Ann Marsh, Ellen O'Connor, Anna Searing, Sidney Taliaferro, and Henrietta Wolcott, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters contain well-wishes for Howland's trip to Europe. A letter from August 10 describes a small earthquake felt in Bozrah, Connecticut. Another letter from Anna Searing discusses the upcoming presidential election as well as a conversation with Frederick Douglass about the immortality of the soul (circa November) Correspondents include Anna Searing and Sidney Taliaferro, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters about Elizabeth Comstock's move to Union Springs. Correspondents include Theodosia Chaplin, Amanda Sanford Hickey, H. Hussey, Ellen O'Connor, Mary Reed, and Lydia King, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from D. A. Pennock describing a meeting with Susan B. Anthony as well as updates to various publications. Correspondents include D.E. Collins, Amanda Sanford Hickey, H. Hussey, Lydia King, Carrie Lacy, Mary Reed, Anna Searing, Harriette Shattuck, and Charlotte Smith, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopy of a pledge against alcohol and narcotics, signed by Howland and many others. Correspondents include Amanda Sanford Hickey, Lydia King, Mary Reed, Anna Searing, and Alfreda Bosworth Withington, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopy of the Call for the First Woman Suffrage Convention in Washington Twenty-One Years Since. Correspondents include Elizabeth Comstock, Phebe Frost, Lydia King, R.J. Laws, Mary Reed, S. Searing, Mary Taliaferro, and Sidney Taliaferro, among others

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Elizabeth Comstock talking about society and Pandita Ramabai (January 30), letters from African American individuals seeking aid for housing or schools, and letters mentioning suffrage meetings. Correspondents include R.J. Laws, John Taliaferro, Anna Searing, Sarah Dolley, Ellen O'Connor, Alfreda Bosworth Withington, and Phebe Coffin, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Alfreda Withington describing recent medical cases and life as a woman physician (February 4), and many letters discussing women's suffrage. Correspondents include Alfreda Bosworth Withington, Anna Searing, D.E. Colins, W.D. Laws, Bettie Urquhart, and Sallie Holley, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters discuss women's suffrage events and ideas. Includes a photocopy of a letter from Susan B. Anthony (July 22). Correspondents include Catherine Helen Spence, Anna Searing, Mary F. Eastman, Eliza Wright Osborne, Bettie Urquhart, A. Gertrude Flanders, Sallie Holley, Amanda T. Jones, Clara Bewick Colby, and Jane Slocum, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters discuss women's suffrage events and ideas. Includes a letter from Booker T. Washington, as principal of the Tuskegee Normal and Industrial Institute, regarding a chapel fund and the Christian Register (November 3). Correspondents include Diana C. Williams, Jane E. Thompson, Ellen S. Mussey, Caroline Lacy, John Taliaferro, Jane Slocum, Serena Myers, Eliza Wright Osborne, Zobedia Alleman, Harriet May Mills, Amanda Sanford Hickey, Bettie Urquhart, Lydia King, Kate Gordon, and Harriet Upton, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters discuss women's suffrage events and ideas. Correspondents include Jean Greenleaf, Mrs. E.G. Draper (Sister Charlotte), T. Taliaferro, and Zobedia Allemen, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters discuss women's suffrage events and ideas. Includes three letters from Booker T. Washington discussing financial issues at Tuskegee and the health of his wife. Correspondents include Eliza Wright Osborne, C.H. Spence, Jane E. Thompson, H.P. Montgomery, Louise Zabriskie, Diana Hirschler, Thamsen Taliaferro, A. Gertrude Flanders, Alfreda Bosworth Withington, Caroline Putnam, Carolyn Fitzhugh, Gleason Sanitarium, Mary Lowe Dickinson, Samuel E. Courtney, Sara Goodyear, Jewish Seaside Home for Invalids, and Anna Searing, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of a letter from George Washington Carver discussing art (December 30, 1897), and a letter from Booker T. Washington (November 23, 1898.) Correspondents include Orra Langhorne, Adella Hunt Logan, among others. Includes the "Lincoln University Herald", Vol. IV No. 3, and "Extracts from the Catechism on Peace and Arbitration of the National Council of Women."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Martha Schofield, Caroline Putnam, Cornelia Spitzer, A. Brown, Adella Hunt Logan, Anne Miller, Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, Orra Langhorne, and Anna Stanton, among others. Includes Ohio State University 22nd Annual Commencement program.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Amanda T. Jones laying out her business plans for a new cannery, including descriptions of the various canning processes, discussions of only employing women, and the use of funds to provide for women in need. Also includes photocopy of a letter from Booker T. Washington (November 30) about the machine shop at the Tuskegee Normal and Industrial Institute. Correspondents include Susan Look Avery, Anna Stanton, Zobedia Alleman, Debbie B. Young, Jane E. Thompson, Clara Colby, Harriet Purvis, Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, Jane Nash, Caroline Putnam, Anne Miller, Calla DeGreene, and Phebe Coffin, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopies of letters from Susan B. Anthony, and a letter from Eliza Anna Grier praising Booker T. Washington (February 25). Some letters relate to building a Girls Hotel at George Junior Republic. Correspondents include William Birdsall Gifford, Calla DeGreene, Eliza Anna Grier, Mary Loines, Debbie B. Young, Anna Stanton, Clara Colby, Anna Rice Powell, Eliza Wright Osborne, Zobedia Alleman, Caroline Putnam, Anne Miller, Lucy Salisbury Doolittle, Harriet May Mills, Jane Nash, Jane E. Thompson, Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, Martha Schofield, Sallie Ely, and Adella Hunt Logan, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopies of letters from Susan B. Anthony. Correspondents include Debbie B. Young, Helen E. Hart, Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, C. Rexford Raymond, Anna Stanton, Caroline Putnam, Harriet May Mills, Lydia King, Lydia Boyer, Adella Hunt Logan, Phebe Coffin, Susan Abbott, Sallie Ely, and Anna Searing, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopies of letters from Lucy E. Anthony and Anna Howard Shaw. Correspondents include Harriet May Mills, Caroline Putnam, Eliza Wright Osborne, Lucy E. Anthony, Phebe Coffin, Anna M. Stanton, Ella Hawley Crossett, and Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas, and to suffrage. Includes a letter from Howland to her niece Isabel, describing a visit with Fanny Jackson Coppin and recounting her Coppin's life and work (March 14). Correspondents include Phebe Coffin, Caroline Putnam, Robert Curtis Ogden, Booker T. Washington, William Henry Holtzclaw, William R. Carter, Harriet May Mills, Zobedia Alleman, Lyman Ward, Jane E. Thompson, Henry D. Davidson, George Eliot Cooley, Adella H. Logan, Anna Stanton, William E. Benson, Susan Abbott, Georgieanna Field Potter, and Anna Howard Shaw, among others. Also includes a pamphlet for The Utica Normal and Industrial Institute.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas, and to suffrage. Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Jane E. Thompson, William R. Carter, Helen Hart, Lyman Ward, Harriet Taylor Upton, Adella H. Logan, Amanda T. Jones, and Anna Searing, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas, and to suffrage. Includes a letter from Adella H. Logan discussing her health concerns and end-of-life wishes (January 5), as well as letters and clippings discussing the death of Susan B. Anthony. Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Anna M. Stanton, Susan Abbott, William J. Edwards, Harriet Taylor Upton, Clara Colby, Alice Stone Blackwell, Amanda Deyo, Jennie Dean, A. Gertrude Flanders, Jane Slocum, Oswald Garrison Villard, Adella H. Logan, Antoinette Brown Blackwell, Henry Davidson, and W.S. Montgomery, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas, suffrage, and mentions of the San Francisco earthquake. Letters from William James Edwards discusses the purchasing of plantations in the South by Northerners and his wish to buy a plantation. Correspondents include Jane E. Thompson, Caroline Putnam, Harriet May Mills, Harriet Taylor Upton, Adella H. Logan, William R. Carter, William Henry Holzclaw, Henry Davidson, William James Edwards, Amanda Deyo, Jennie Dean, Lyman Ward, and William Taylor, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas, suffrage, and the political campaigns and number of Mormon women voters in Idaho. Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Eliza Wright Osborne, William R. Carter, Harriet May Mills, Anna Stanton, Jane Slocum, Harriet Taylor Upton, Clara Colby, Fred Thomas Dubois, and Laura Redden Searing, among others. Includes a pamphlet for the Cheyney Summer School.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to the school at Manassas and suffrage. Correspondents include R.J. Laws, Martin Menafee, Anna Stanton, Martha Schofield, Jane E. Thompson, Rebecca Taliaferro, Katherine Munhall, and Henry D. Davidson, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to Kowaliga School in Alabama. Includes a photocopy of a letter from Anna Howard Shaw describing the segregation she saw on a train from Missouri to Arkansas (August 3). Correspondents include Booker T. Washington, Mala Taliaferro, Henry D. Davidson, Adella Hunt Logan, Caroline Putnam, Martin Menafee, Debbie B. Young, Sallie Ely, William R. Carter, William James Edwards, Maria Sterling, Jennie Dean, Eliza Wright Osborne, Alfreda Bosworth Withingon, Oswald Garrison Villard, Rachel Foster Avery, Anna Stanton, A. Gertrude Flanders, Jenkin Lloyd Jones, Harriet May Mills, William H. Holtzclaw, Anna Howard Shaw, Maria Ogilvie Gordon, Leslie Pinckney Hill, and Katherine Munhall, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes copies of articles about segregation at Berea College and quotes from the president of Harvard University about segregation; letters relating to Manassas Industrial School; and a photocopy of a letter and photograph from The American Mission School in Bombay, India, of an Indian boy named Babu Lale in need of sponsorship and support. Correspondents include William Frost, Novella Middleton Nelson, Caroline Putnam, Martin Menafee, Harriet May Mills, William H. Holtzclaw, Anna Howard Shaw, Rebecca Taliaferro, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Emma M. Gillett, William E. Benson, Eliza M. Hussey, Katherine Munhall, and Oswald Garrison Villard, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to Manassas Industrial School and Kowaliga School. Includes a photocopy of a "Report on Franchise" by Howland, addressing suffrage. Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Oswald Garrison Villard, Henry D. Davidson, Anna Stanton, William R. Carter, Alfreda Withington, Harriet Taylor Upton, Debbie B. Young, Rebecca Taliaferro, William Logan, De Alva Alexander, William E. Benson, Alice L. White, Katherine Munhall, Lyman Ward, Harriet Stanton Blatch, Martin Menafee, Adella Hunt Logan, Clara Colby, Eliza Wright Osborne, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Jane E. Thompon, Annie E. Merritt, William H. Holtzclaw, Babu Lale, Kate M. Gordon, Jennie Dean, Kate M. Gordon, and Anna Searing, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters relate to Manassas Industrial School and the fire at Kowaliga School. Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Jennie Dean, Booker T. Washington, Rebecca Taliaferro, Harriet Taylor Upton, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Lyman Ward, Alice L. White, Annie E. Merritt, Martha Schofield, William R. Carter, William H. Holtzclaw, Harriet May Mills, William E. Benson, Eliza Wright Osborne, Debbie B. Young, Rebecca Taliaferro, Henry D. Davidson, Clara Colby, A.W. Nicholson, A. Gertrude Flanders, Agnes Salmund, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Alfred H. Love, Charlotte Hancock, Katherine Munhall, Ella Crossett, Annie E. Merritt, Elizabeth Evelyn Wright, William J. Edwards, and Sallie Ely, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include William J. Edwards, Wilson L. Gill, Martin Menafee, Rebecca Taliaferro, Debbie B. Young, Cornelia Bowen, Emma M. Gillett, Jennie Dean, A.W. Nicholson, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Charlotte Hancock, Alfred H. Love, Harriet May Mills, William H. Holtzclaw, Eliza Wright Osborne, William R. Carter, Katherine Munhall, Clara Colby, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Booker T. Washington, Jennie McKinney, Mrs. D.B. Williams, Adella Hunt Logan, Martha Schofield, Lyman Ward, and Harriet Taylor Upton, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Seraphine Dewey at the American Mission in Mardin, Turkey, about the state of the orphanage, two children that Howland sponsored, and photographs of the children and the mission yard (March 14). The letter also discusses men between 20-25 trying to escape conscription if they marry orphans, and how many young girls were recently married across the city. Correspondents include William E. Benson, Ellen Mussey, Jane E Thompson, Rebecca Taliaferro, A. Gertrude Flanders, Cornelia Bowen, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Caroline Putnam, Anna Stanton, and Jennie Dean, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Seraphine Dewey at the American Mission in Mardin, Turkey, about the death of Esleem, a woman Howland had sponsored, and Wordie, who had recently married. Includes photographs of Esleem and Wordie (September 25). Correspondents include Caroline Putnam, Emily C. Wheeler, Mary McLeod Bethune, Harriet Taylor Upton, Sallie Ely, Henry D. Davidson, Oswald Garrison Villard, Booker T. Washington, Emanuel M. McDuffie, A.W. Nicholson, Leslie Pinckney Smith, W.S. Montgomery, Emma M. Gillett, William J. Edwards, Martin Menafee, and William R. Carter, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from Jane E. Thompson about the death of Jennie Dean (May),. Correspondents include Laurence C. Jones, Arthur W. Mitchell, Caroline Putnam, Emma M. Gillett, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Adella H. Logan, Nettie Lovisa White, Eliza R. Whiting, Cornelia Bowen, A. Eugene Thompson, William Frost, Anna Stanton, Jane E. Thompson, William R. Holtzclaw, A.W. Nicholson, Eliza Hussey, Clara Colby, Katherine Munhall, William R. Carter, Emily C. Wheeler, Henry D. Davidson, Leslie Pinckney Hill, Harriet May Mills, Martin Menafee, Clara MacNoughton, Jewette Lane, Alfred J. Griffin, Harriet Taylor Upton, and Oswald Garrison Villard, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include William E. Benson, Cornelia Bowen, Arabella Carter, William H. Holtzclaw, William James, Addie Jayne, Laurence C. Jones, Martin Menafee, A.W. Nicholson, and Caroline Putnam, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include William E. Benson, M.C. Calhoun, Seraphine Dewey, Sallie Ely, William H. Holtzclaw, Laurence C. Jones, Adella H. Logan, Martin Menafee, A.W. Nicholson, Caroline Putnam, Anna Howard Shaw, and Lyman Ward, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Some letters address the death of Booker T. Washington in 1915. Correspondents include Henry D. Davidson, Ernest T. Eaton, William J. Edwards, Josephine Eschenbrenner, Alfred J. Griffin, William H. Holtzclaw, Addie Jayne, William James, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Martin Menafee, Katherine Munhall, A.W. Nicholson, Ethel Phelps, Lyman Ward, and Emily C. Wheeler, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photocopy of a letter from Anna Howard Shaw calling the Silent Sentinels (a group of women who protested in front of the White House for women's suffrage) the "silliest thing a sane body of women ever did" (January 31). Correspondents include William R. Carter, A. Gertrude Flanders, Emma M. Gillett, Laurence C. Jones, Martin Menafee, Robert R. Moton, Mary B. Owen, Emily C. Wheeler, Alice L. White, Florence E. Wilbur, and T.C. Williams, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Alice Stone Blackwell, A. Gertrude Flanders, William James, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Martin Menafee, Edna L. Nicholson, Mary B. Owen, Florence E. Wilbur, and Georgia Washington, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters celebrate the passing of national Prohibition and mentions of influenza. Correspondents include William J. Edwards, Thomas R. Husk, Addie Jayne, Martin Menafee, Harriet A. Mills, Katherine Munhall, A.W. Nicholson, Mary B. Owen, A.S. Steele, and Florence E. Wilbur, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include William J. Edwards, William James, Emanuel M. McDuffie, Martin Menafee, Harriet A. Mills, Robert R. Moton, J.R. Russell, Lyman Ward, and Alice L. White, among others. Also includes Volume 9, Number 1 of "The Worker."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Mary E. Craigie, Henry D. Davidson, Katherine Munhall, A.W. Nicholson, Mary B. Owen, A. Eugene Thomson, Lyman Ward, Georgia Washinton, Emily C. Wheeler, and Alice L. White, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Alice Stone Blackwell, William Hutchins, Doris James, Addie Jayne, Laurence C. Jones, Sanford G. Lyon, A.W. Nicholson, Maegusta Smith, Evie Lee Spencer, Georgia Washington, Emily C. Wheeler, and Alice L. White, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include William J. Hutchins, Novella M. Nelson, A.W. Nicholson, Margaret Scattergood, John H. Toulson, and Georgia Washington, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Lucy E. Anthony, Henry D. Davidson, William J. Edwards, Martin Menafee, Robert R. Moton, Katherine Munhall, A.W. Nicholson, Harry Simms, William C. Taylor, Emily C. Wheeler, and Alice L. White, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Zobedia Alleman, Alice Stone Blackwell, Catherine Breshkovsky, Esther B. George, William James, Katharine E. Letchworth, Laura C. Letchworth, Katherine Munhall, Novella M. Nelson, Marian O'Harrow, and Emily A. Ransom, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Cornelia Bowen, William James, Thomas E. Jones, A.W. Nicholson, George M. Sampson, and Georgia Washington, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include Henry D. Davidson, Jennie Dean, Amanda Deyo, Sallie Ely, Sallie Holley, and J.R. Jackson, Caroline Putnam, Mary Reed, Anna H. Searing, Anna Stanton, Sidney Taliaferro, Jane E. Thompson, Babu B. Yale, Emily C. Wheeler, and Mrs. D.B. Williams, among others. Also includes "The Amanda T. Jones Association recreant to no trust".

did.unittitle: "Historical Sketch of Friends in Cayuga County" by Emily Howland
"Emily Howland: An Unforgettable Person" by Bessie Cudworth and Miles Jacobs
Poster for "50 Years of Freedom: Northern Virginia Negro Fair at the Manassas Industrial School for Colored Youth"
Series II. Howland Family Correspondence and Papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains correspondence between members of Emily Howland's family, including Phebe Tallcot, Joseph Tallcot, Charles Augustus Howland, Slocum Howland, Isabel Howland, and others. Many folders contain photocopies of correspondence held by other institutions or individuals.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York and Auxiliary Bible Associations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York, Wilburites, and correspondence with Mahlon Day. Also includes a letter from William Orville Duvall to Slocum Howland discussing the transport of an enslaved person to Duvall's house in Port Byron, with Duvall advising he be armed with a revolver in case of an attempted arrest.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York, and a letter from J. Letchworth describing slavery in Virginia.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter to Lady Alsop (sender unknown) about the anti-slavery movement in Ohio and the Anti-Slavery Standard, the Womens Loyal League Emancipation Petition, and the Civil War. Also includes a letter from Sallie Holley (January 16, 1866) requesting donations for the Anti-Slavery Festival in Boston.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes discussion of meetings of the Society of Friends in central New York. ; a letter from Sallie Holley (January 5, 1869) written on the back of a Thirty-Fifth National Anti-Slavery Subscription Anniversary pamphlet; and queries from the Hector Monthly Meeting of Women Friends, 1870.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Emily Howland to her mother mentioning Harriet Tubman (1895).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes travel documents from France from Isabel Howland's trip.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Largely from Isabel Howland to Charles and Alice.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters from Isabel Howland describing Paris and the war.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes poems, sermons, and writing compositions by members of the Howland family.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Several pages of notes seem to be in letter form and addressed to George.

did.unittitle: Humphrey Howland estate documents and receipts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopy of his will.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many articles relate to Emily Howland.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photocopy.

did.unittitle: Empty envelopes addressed to various Howland family members
Diary of William Howland's trip to Bermuda
Howland, Alsop, Hoxie, and Chase family photographs
Album pages with photographs of Emily Howland, family home, family photographs, correspondence, and clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Album created by Peter A. Curtiss.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters are to Sallie Holley and discuss current political events, the Quaker church, and women's rights. One letter describes meeting Samuel J. May (1851) and prison reform.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters discuss current political and social events. Includes letters from Gerrit Smith to Sallie Holley (June 24, 1865 and September 9, 1867) as well as letters to Sallie from others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters discuss current political and social events. Most letters are from Putnam.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters discuss current political and social events. Most letters are from Putnam.

did.unittitle: Letters from Putnam to E.S. Miller
Series IV. Microfilm copies of select material in Series I, II, and III

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Refer to the published microfilm guide in box 6, folder 13 for further description.

unittitle.extref: Link to a copy of the published microfilm guide. (4 MB PDF)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Some of the material microfilmed is held at Swarthmore College.
